mnemonics.title: SLOW FAST MIDDLE
mnemonics.content: SLOW AND FAST POINTER FOR MIDDLE: SLOW moves 1, FAST moves 2. When FAST hits end, SLOW is at middle.

mnemonics.title: TORTISE HARE CYCLE
mnemonics.content: FLOYD'S CYCLE DETECTION: Think of tortoise and hare racing. If there's a circle, faster hare will eventually lap the tortoise.

mnemonics.title: LEAD THEN FOLLOW
mnemonics.content: NTH FROM END: LEAD pointer goes N nodes ahead, then FOLLOW and LEAD move together. When LEAD hits end, FOLLOW is at Nth position.

mnemonics.title: PREV CUR NEXT REVERSE
mnemonics.content: REVERSE LINKED LIST: PREV = NULL, CUR = head. Store NEXT, reverse CUR's link to PREV, then advance PREV and CUR.

acronyms.expansion: First In First Out - queue behavior in list operations like enqueue
acronyms.usage: Used when adding elements to end and removing from front

acronyms.expansion: Last In First Out - stack behavior, can be implemented using linked list
acronyms.usage: Linked list can act as stack by push/pop from head
Short form used in code and documentation
Linear structure with next pointers only
Last node points to first node

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a race track where one runner (slow) runs 1 lap while another (fast) runs 2 laps. When fast finishes race, slow is at halfway point (middle).
Two people walking on a circular path - one fast, one slow. The fast one will eventually meet the slow one from behind.
Two fingers on a ruler. Place first finger at Nth mark from start, then move both fingers together until first finger reaches end.
Two train cars joined together - connect the coupler (next pointer) of the last car of first train to the first car of second train.

analogies.analogy: Like a treasure hunt where each clue (node) points to the next location. You must follow the chain to reach the end.
A circular road where if you keep driving, you'll pass the same landmarks again and again.
Unlinking and re-linking paper clips in a chain. You disconnect each link and reconnect it in the opposite direction.
Instead of filling an entire warehouse (matrix) with empty boxes (zeros), only store the items that actually exist with their location tags.
